coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated MoSe2 photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction in the UV–vis-NIR photon energy range

coredata.dc:description: Developing whole spectrum responsive photocatalysts is one of greatly significant subjects from the viewpoint of using solar energy. Here we have firstly developed a carbon quantum dots-decorated MoSe2 photocatalyst that is active in the whole spectrum of solar light for Cr(VI) reduction. The Cr(VI) reduction rate of higher than 99% has been achieved with 1wt.% nanocarbon emersions under UV, visible and near-infrared irradiation at 180min. Improvement is attributed to its excellent light absorption, efficient charge separation, and the light down converting effect of carbon quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) MoSe2 and (b) MC-1; (c) low-magnification and (d) high-magnification HRTEM images of MC-1.
XRD patterns of MoSe2 and MC-1.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Mo 3d, (b) Se 3d, and (c) C 1s for MC-1.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts, and (b) PL spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) UV–vis-NIR diffuses absorption spectra, (b) Nyquist plots, (c) transient photocurrent responses under visible light irradiation, and (d) time resolved PL spectra of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts.
(a) Visible light photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by (a) MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts, and (b) MC-1 under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated MoSe2 photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction in the UV–vis-NIR photon energy range

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots-decorated MoSe2 hybrid photocatalyst displays excellent photocatalytic activity in the reduction of Cr(VI) under UV and visible even NIR light irradiation.

Developing whole spectrum responsive photocatalysts is one of greatly significant subjects from the viewpoint of using solar energy. Here we have firstly developed a carbon quantum dots-decorated MoSe2 photocatalyst that is active in the whole spectrum of solar light for Cr(VI) reduction. The Cr(VI) reduction rate of higher than 99% has been achieved with 1wt.% nanocarbon emersions under UV, visible and near-infrared irradiation at 180min. Improvement is attributed to its excellent light absorption, efficient charge separation, and the light down converting effect of carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: A great deal of pollutants directly discards from manufacturing process, bringing about serious threat to local environment and human health. Heavy metal ions such as hexavalent chromium, Cr(VI), are highly toxic and can cause the cancer, perforation of nasal septum and kidney damage. The reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) is considered as an efficient measure to remove Cr(VI), because less toxic Cr(III) can be readily precipitated to form the Cr(OH)3 in aqueous solution [1].

As a potential, low cost and green method, semiconductor photocatalysis has attracted much interest due to its wide applications in the production of hydrogen [2], degradation of organic pollutants [3–5], and reduction of heavy metal ions and gases [6,7]. The limited light absorption range and conversion efficiency of semiconductor photocatalysts are major concerns in photocatalysis. Intensive research has been carried out to use the ultraviolet (UV) or visible light [8–14]. Although near-infrared (NIR) light accounts for more than 50% of the solar energy, few reports have been found to effectively utilize the NIR light. Therefore, exploring high efficiency and whole spectrum (UV, visible and NIR) responsive semiconductor photocatalysts is still a challenging issue.

Recently, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have attracted great deal of interest owing to their original properties [15] and wide applications in hydrogen evolution [16], lithium/sodium batteries [17] and photocatalysis [18,19]. Among these TMDs, MoSe2 is an indirect band gap semiconductor with bandgap of 1.2eV in the bulk form, and has also attracted interest as photovoltaic and photocatalytic materials [20]. Up to now, theoretical and experimental studies have only focused on the visible light photocatalytic degradation of dye and splitting of water using MoSe2 [21]. Unique structures of MoSe2 such as highly ordered mesoporous crystalline constructed by a nanocasting strategy using hard template has been demonstrated with excellent visible light photocatalytic activity in the degradation of Rhodamine B [20]. Unfortunately, few efforts have been made on the UV and NIR light photocatalytic activity of MoSe2.

In photocatalysis, it is well known that the quick recombination of charge carriers will lead to the relatively low photocatalytic activity. Many studies indicate that designing hybrid materials could offer the driving forces to facilitate the transfer of charge carriers. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have acted as the matrix to support the semiconductors, leading to the improvement of photocatalytic activity, which is due to the broad band optical absorption and strong photoluminescence (PL) emission [22–24]. CQDs-based composite, such as Fe2O3/CQDs, BiOX (X=Br, Cl)/CQDs, TiO2/CQDs, Cu2O/CQDs, exhibit a good photocatalytic activity [25–27]. In photocatalytic process, CQDs as electron-acceptor material can promote the transfer and reduce the recombination of charge carriers due to the electronic interaction between semiconductors and CQDs [23,28,29]. Furthermore, the up-converting properties of CQDs make the CQDs-based composites can absorb the longer wavelength light, and emit the shorter wavelength light, which can be effectively absorbed by photocatalyst to generate more electron-hole pairs [30,31]. All these contribute to the excellent photocatalytic activity of CQDs-based composites. Therefore, hybrid photocatalysts based on MoSe2 and CQDs should also show enhanced photocatalytic activity under UV, Visible even NIR light irradiation compared to pure MoSe2. Unfortunately, little has yet been known about the photocatalytic activity of the MoSe2-CQDs composites, especially under UV, Visible and NIR light irradiation.

In this work, we report discovery of the broad spectrum responsive carbon quantum dots-decorated MoSe2 (MC) hybrid photocatalysts that were synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. The detailed experimental procedure is described in Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI†). The as-synthesized MC hybrid photocatalysts with 0.5, 1, 2 and 4wt.% CQDs by changing the amount of sucrose were labeled as MC-0.5, MC-1, MC-2 and MC-4, respectively. The MC hybrid photocatalysts show excellent photo-absorption in the whole solar-light regime and improved photocatalytic activity in the reduction of Cr(VI) compared with pure MoSe2 under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation.

The FESEM images of MoSe2 and MC-1 are shown in Fig.1 (a) and (b). It can be found that MoSe2 displays a sheet nanostructure. The morphologies of MC-1, MC-2 and MC-4 (not shown here) are similar to that of MC-0.5. As shown in Fig.1(b), CQDs are well distributed in MoSe2 sheets in the MC hybrid photocatalyst. In addition, it can be observed that there is no change in the morphology of MoSe2 in the MC hybrid photocatalyst (MC-0.5). The low-magnification and high-magnification HRTEM images of MC-1 are shown in Fig.1(c) and (d), respectively. It can be observed that the CQDs contact with MoSe2 sheets in the MC hybrid photocatalyst. Fig. S1(a) and (b) show the HRTEM image and the particle size distribution of CQDs, respectively. It can be found that CQDs with the size of 10–20nm are quite uniform and monodisperse. The interlayer spacing of 0.68nm can be ascribed to the (002) direction of MoSe2, which is slightly larger than that of bulk MoSe2 (0.64nm). Connecting to the MoSe2, the lattice fringe of 0.32nm is ascribed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [32]. The selected area electron diffraction pattern (Fig. S2) exhibits clear diffraction rings, indicating a high crystallinity of MoSe2.

The XRD patterns of MoSe2 and MC-1 are shown in Fig. 2 . All the peaks in the diffraction pattern of pure MoSe2 can be indexed to the hexagonal 2H-MoSe2 (JCPDS: 29-0914). CQDs exhibits a weak broad peak centered at 24°, which is attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [28] (Fig. S3). The main diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of MC-1 can be ascribed to the hexagonal 2H-MoSe2, indicating that the presence of MoSe2 in the MC hybrid photocatalyst. It is noted that no typical diffraction peaks of carbon species are observed, which may be due to the low amount of CQDs in MC-1 hybrid photocatalyst [27,33,34].

Fig. 3 (a–c) shows the high resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d, Se 3d and C 1s. The Mo 3d spectrum of MC-1 is fitted into two peaks. The peaks at 228.7eV and 232.4eV correspond to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively, indicating the reduction of Mo6+ to Mo4+. The peak at 55eV corresponds to the Se 3d3/2 [35]. The C 1s spectrum of MC-1 is fitted into three peaks. The main peak of C 1s at 284.6eV is ascribed to the CC bond with sp2 orbital [36]. The peaks at 286.5 and 288.4eV correspond to CO and CO, respectively [27,34]. The atom contents of Mo, Se and C are 24.45, 10.06 and 65.49at%, respectively. All of these results indicate that the CQDs are successfully incorporated into the MoSe2.

Fig.4 (a) displays the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts. All of them show type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop. The BET specific surface areas, pore sizes and pore volumes of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts are listed in Table S1. The results show that the specific surface area decreases with the increase of CQDs content, which is not in agreement with photocatalytic activity. Therefore, it does not play a decisive role in the photocatalytic activity of MC hybrid photocatalysts. The pore volume and pore size of MC-1 are larger than those of other samples, which can influence the fast transport of Cr(VI) and allow more Cr(VI) to enter into the MC-1, and thus is beneficial to the photocatalytic activity [37]. Fig.4(b) shows the PL emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. It can be observed that as-prepared CQDs have an obvious down-converted property, and the PL peak correspondingly shifts from 438 to 530nm when the excitation wavelength is in a range of 355–485nm, which falls in the absorption range of MoSe2 well. Therefore, CQDs can absorb the shorter wavelength light, and emit the visible light, which can excite the MoSe2 to generate more electron-hole pairs, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

The UV–vis-NIR diffuse absorption spectra of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts are shown in Fig.5 (a). It can be found that pure MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts display strong absorption in the whole light region. Therefore, they should exhibit good photocatalytic activity under UV, visible light and even NIR light irradiation. Fig.5(b) shows the typical Nyquist plots of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts. The semicircle results from the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and constant phase element (CPE) at the photocatalyst/electrolyte interface. The inclined line is ascribed to the Warburg impedance ZW, which corresponds to the ion diffusion process in the electrolyte. The inset in Fig.5(b) shows the corresponding equivalent circuit. The value of R ct decreases with the increase of CQDs content, which indicates that the introduction of CQDs can inhibit the recombination of charge carries. However, when the CQDs content is further increased, the value of R ct will increase. It is because that excessive CQDs as a recombination center can promote the recombination of charge carries [38].

In order to further investigate the charge transfer behavior of MC hybrid photocatalysts, the time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) and photoelectronchemical measurements were carried out. Fig.5(c) shows the TRPL spectra of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts. The decay curves are fitted by exponentials to obtain the decay time. The average lifetimes of charge carriers are calculated to be 0.17, 0.20, 0.24, 0.18 and 0.18ns for MoSe2, MC-0.5, MC-1, MC-2 and MC-4, respectively. The results indicate that the lifetime of MC hybrid photocatalysts increases with the increase of CQDs content, and MC-1 shows the longest lifetime. Fig.5(d) shows the transient photocurrent responses of MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts under visible light irradiation. When the light is switched off, the photocurrent quickly decreases to zero, indicating the recombination of charge carriers. Longer lifetime and higher photocurrent show more efficient charge transfer process, which can improve the photocatalytic activity [12]. All results are in agreement with the EIS results.

The photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation was used to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared MoSe2 and MC hybrid photocatalysts. The blank photocatalytic activity without catalyst under visible light irradiation was also investigated, as shown in Fig. S4. It can be observed that the concentration of Cr(VI) is hardly reduced under visible light irradiation in the absence of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig.6 (a), MC hybrid photocatalysts show excellent photocatalytic activity compared to pure MoSe2, and the photocatalytic activity of MC hybrid photocatalysts is dependent on the proportion of CQDs. The Cr(VI) reduction rate for MoSe2 is 69% at 180min. When CQDs is introduced into MoSe2, the Cr(VI) reduction rates reach to 82% and 99% at 180min for MC-0.5 and MC-1, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic activity is due to the excellent light absorption in whole light spectrum, the efficient charge transfer, and the light down-converting effect of CQDs. However, the Cr(VI) reduction rates are 81% and 76% at 180min for MC-2 and MC-4, respectively, which should be ascribed to their increased recombination of charge carries and relatively lower light harvesting [28]. The values of rate constants (k) can be obtained directly from the fitted straight-line plots of ln(C/C 0) vs. reaction time t (Fig. S5(a)), which follow the order: MC-1 (0.026min−1)>MC-0.5 (0.009min−1)>MC-2 (0.009min−1)>MC-4 (0.008min−1)>MoSe2 (0.007min−1). Therefore, MC-1 exhibits the highest Cr(VI) reduction rate under visible light irradiation. Photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) using MC-1 was also studied under UV and NIR light irradiation (Fig.5(b)). The Cr(VI) reduction rates reach 100% and 99% at 180min under UV and NIR light irradiation, respectively. The values of k under UV and NIR light irradiation are 0.009min−1, as shown in Fig. S5(b). Therefore, MC hybrid photocatalysts exhibit a good photocatalytic activity not only under visible light irradiation but also under UV and NIR light irradiation.

The photo-stability of photocatalysts is very important for practical application. Therefore, the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) with three cycles using MC-1 was also studied under visible light irradiation (Fig. S6(a)). It can be found that the Cr(VI) reduction rate only insignificant decreases. Moreover, the crystal structure of MC-1 after the photocatalytic reaction was characterized by XRD (Fig. S6(b)). The results show that the crystal structure of MC-1 has not changed before and after the reaction, demonstrating that the MC hybrid photocatalyst is fairly stability.

To analyze the photocatalytic mechanism, controlled experiments were performed through adding the electrons scavenger (K2S2O8 and KBrO3) in the photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. S7, the Cr(VI) reduction rate decreases obviously with the addition of K2S2O8 or KBrO3, indicating that the photo-induced electrons play a key role in the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) process [39]. In the photocatalytic process, the photo-generated electrons can reduce the Cr(VI) to produce Cr(III). Meanwhile, the hole can oxidize the water to form oxygen in the photocatalytic process, which has been confirmed by the gas chromatograph [40,41]. The major reaction steps are summarized as follows:
MoSe
2
+ h ν →
h
+
+
e
-
Cr
2
O
7
2 -
+ 14
H
+
+ 6
e
-
→ 2
Cr
3 +
+ 7
H
2
O
2
H
2
O + 4
h
+
→
O
2
+ 4
H
+

In summary, a series of UV–vis-NIR active MC hybrid photocatalysts with different weight ratios of nanocarbon have been successfully synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. The photocatalytic activity of MC hybrid photocatalysts is superior to that of pure MoSe2, and the Cr(VI) reduction rate of higher than 99% has been achieved with 1wt.% nanocarbon emersions under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation at 180min. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is ascribed to the triple effects of the enhanced light absorption, efficient charge transfer, and light down-converting effect resulting from the introduction of CDQs. Moreover, it is interesting to find that the photo-induced electrons has a significant influence on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) process. The current work could provide a general guiding line for designing novel UV–vis-NIR light photocatalyst to harvest solar energy, which would be found promising applications in environmental purification and energy conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21401180, 11302089 and 51402274), Foundation of Xuzhou city (KC15SM029) and Special Fund for Agro-scientific Research in the Public Interest (No. 201303011) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.10.077.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: W. Wei, Y. Dai, C.w. Niu, B.B. Huang, Sci. Rep. 5 (2015) 17578.
